fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the new service wiring toolkit shipped. Basically the idea is that right now every dev on the team is manually newing up objects, passing deps through constructors by hand, and copy-pasting cache logic everywhere — it's a mess and we keep having bugs where the wrong instance gets reused (or doesn't get reused when it should). We need a single container thing where you register stuff once and just ask for it later. Should handle the usual cases: pre-built objects, factory callables, closure-based factories, and also that abstract factory pattern we used in the config module last quarter (you know the one). It also needs to support aliasing so you can ask for an interface name and get the concrete impl back, and aliases should chain. There should be a way to wrap or decorate objects after they're built — similar to what the initializer hooks do in that login flow we shipped — so downstream teams can bolt on behavior without touching core. Oh and if someone tries to reconfigure something that's already registered it should blow up with a clean error, not a silent overwrite. Also need that child-container / plugin-registry pattern for type-validated extension points. Finally, there's a PSR-compatible wrapper layer so third-party containers can be plugged in. Make sure cycle detection is in there for aliases. Rough deadline is end of sprint.

One extra pass on details the team asked about: by default, every registered entry should be treated as shared, so if you call get() twice for the same identifier you get back the exact same cached object instance unless that specific entry says otherwise or the caller uses the explicit build path. And yes, each registration can carry its own sharing flag. If that flag is present, it wins over the container-wide default, so we can have a globally non-shared container with a few entries still cached, or the other way around.

On the auto-wiring side, if constructor inspection hits a required typed parameter and that type is not registered, the output needs to be exactly error=service_not_found on one line and detail=unresolvable_typed_dependency on the next. Same idea for required scalar params with no default value — they should normalize to the equivalent error behavior too.

For the plugin manager / constrained child container bit, normal lookups should cache the same way the parent container does. But if a get() call comes in with options, it needs to skip the cache, build a fresh object, and pass those options through to the factory. So two optioned lookups should produce two distinct instances even if sharing is turned on. Same general rule for the explicit build path too: build() with an options array always makes a fresh object no matter what the sharing rules say, and two back-to-back build() calls for the same identifier should return two distinct instances. The options map goes straight into the factory so the object can actually see them.

Also confirming the wrapper layer behavior: the PSR-compatible decorator should just forward has() and get() straight through to the wrapped container, and it also needs a dedicated accessor for the wrapped container itself so callers can confirm they’re looking at the same underlying instance. For a known identifier, the output should be exactly has=yes, get_id=<the identifier>, same_wrapped_container=yes.

And just to be explicit about supported registration shapes, we still need all four: a prebuilt service registered directly, a direct factory using a factory class instance, a closure factory using an anonymous callable/closure, and the abstract factory fallback when there’s no specific match. All four should go through the same sharing behavior and the same lifecycle hook flow. That includes the ConfigAbstractFactory (Feature 2) which reads a dependency_map configuration key to recursively resolve and inject constructor dependencies, as described in feature2_1_configured_dependency_factory.json and feature2_2_configured_dependency_errors.json, plus the delegator and initializer lifecycle hooks described in Feature 1.5 (feature1_5_lifecycle_hooks.json): delegators wrap the factory call, initializers run post-creation, both mutate the final resolved object and are tested via the delegator_initializer operation.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with operation and service-container fields) -> stdout service manager result lines

trigger_keywords: how do alias chains resolve
multi-hop alias lookup
alias pointing to another alias
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Aliases are resolved recursively. If alias 'bar' points to 'foo' and 'foo' points to 'optioned_object', then getting 'bar' ultimately returns the same shared instance as getting 'optioned_object' directly. The has() existence check also follows the full chain.

trigger_keywords: cyclic alias behavior
alias cycle error format
what happens when an alias points to itself
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If aliases form a cycle (including a self-referential alias like 'a' -> 'a'), the container must detect it during configuration and report a normalized error. The output must be exactly: error=cyclic_alias on one line and detail=alias_cycle_detected on the next. No language runtime exception names or stack trace details should appear.

trigger_keywords: override protection error format
what error is thrown when re-registering an existing entry
modification not allowed message
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Attempting to overwrite an already-configured entry without enabling override permission must produce: error=modification_not_allowed on one line and detail=unspecified on the next. If allow_override is explicitly enabled before the mutation, the operation succeeds and must output allow_override=yes, mutation_applied=yes, and the resolved object kind.

trigger_keywords: delegator and initializer execution order
lifecycle hooks decoration order
how are post-creation initializers different from delegators
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Delegators wrap the creation call itself — each delegator receives a callback it must invoke to obtain the object, allowing it to modify inputs or outputs. Initializers run after the object is fully created; they receive the container and the created object and can mutate or augment it. Both contribute observable properties to the final resolved object, and the result is cached on subsequent get() calls.

trigger_keywords: configure method returns what
does configure return the container instance
chaining configure calls
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The configure() method must return the same container instance it was called on. Callers can verify this by checking that the reference returned is identical to the original container reference (same_on_second_get style identity check).

trigger_keywords: configured dependency factory error shapes
what errors does the dependency map factory emit
missing config service error detail
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The configured dependency factory normalizes configuration problems into: error=service_not_created plus a detail line whose value matches the config shape problem name (e.g., detail=missing_config_service). Recognized shape problems include: missing_config_service, missing_dependency_map, non_map_config, unavailable_dependency_maps, non_list_dependency_declarations, and non_string_dependency_entries.

trigger_keywords: reflection factory cannot create non-class
what makes an entry not constructible via inspection
inaccessible constructor constructibility
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The constructor inspection factory reports can_create=no for any identifier that does not resolve to a constructible class — this includes plain non-class identifiers and classes whose constructor is not publicly accessible. Classes that exist and have no constructor (or an empty public constructor) are considered constructible and produce can_create=yes.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

are there public test cases
what test cases are provided

what features have test cases

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: full task scope
all public features
semantic equivalence
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Full Laminas ServiceManager surface: service retrieval, set_service operation, override protection, alias resolution and cyclic aliases, factories with build options, sharing rules, delegators and initializers, configured abstract factory can_create/error cases, reflection factory can_create/create/error cases, plugin manager behavior, PSR decorator, and container configuration.

trigger_keywords: dispatcher contract
public test cases
same tests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Route by top-level operation, not action. Valid operation values include service_retrieval, build_with_options, override_protection, alias_resolution, cyclic_alias, delegator_initializer, sharing_rules, config_abstract_factory_can_create, config_abstract_factory_error, reflection_factory_can_create, reflection_factory_create, reflection_factory_error, plugin_manager, psr_decorator, configure_container, and set_service. The nested action=set_service appears only inside the override-protection/setup scenario when present.

trigger_keywords: exact outputs
public test contract
stdout authority
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: laminas operation dispatcher
not action
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes only by top-level operation. Do not route by a top-level action field. Valid operation values include service_retrieval, sharing_rules, build_with_options, alias_resolution, lifecycle hooks, configuration/mutation, override_protection, cyclic_alias, configured dependency factory operations, reflection factory operations, plugin_manager, psr_decorator, and set_service where represented by public cases.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that child-container / plugin-registry pattern for type-validated extension points
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The AbstractPluginManager (Feature 4) described in feature4_1_plugin_manager_validation_and_caching.json, which is a constrained child container that validates resolved objects against an expected type and supports cached lookups plus options-based cache bypass

context_pointers.vague_reference: PSR-compatible wrapper layer so third-party containers can be plugged in
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The ContainerDecorator (Feature 5) described in feature5_1_container_decorator.json, which wraps any PSR-11-compatible container and forwards has() and get() while exposing the wrapped instance via a dedicated accessor

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but it's outside what I have visibility into from a product perspective. I'd recommend checking the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the detailed spec in start.md — the engineering team encoded the exact expected behavior there. If you can point me to a specific output line you're unsure about I can try to clarify the business intent.

feature1_1_service_retrieval.json.description: Resolves configured entries from direct instances, factories, closures, and abstract factories, and reports whether the same entry is cached between repeated lookups.

feature1_2_sharing_rules.json.description: Applies default and per-entry sharing rules when the same configured entry is requested more than once.

feature1_3_build_with_options.json.description: Creates a fresh object with caller-supplied options using the explicit build path instead of retrieving a cached shared entry.

feature1_4_alias_resolution.json.description: Follows alias chains when checking and retrieving entries, returning the same shared target object through every alias.

feature1_5_lifecycle_hooks.json.description: Runs delegators around object creation and initializers after creation so the resolved object contains all externally visible contributions.

feature1_6_configuration_and_mutation.js.description: Adds and changes registrations after construction through configuration and mutation APIs, including factories, aliases, delegators, initializers, and sharing rules.

feature1_7_override_protection.json.description: Rejects mutation of an already configured entry unless override permission has been explicitly enabled.

feature1_8_cyclic_alias_detection.json.description: Detects cycles in alias definitions during configuration and reports a normalized alias-cycle error instead of looping.

feature2_1_configured_dependency_factory.description: Uses an explicit dependency map to decide whether an object can be created and to assemble objects with zero, simple, or nested dependencies.

feature2_2_configured_dependency_errors..description: Reports normalized errors when the explicit dependency map is missing or has a shape that cannot describe dependencies.

feature3_1_reflection_factory_capability.description: Determines whether automatic constructor inspection can create a requested entry based on whether it is a constructible class.

feature3_2_reflection_factory_creation.j.description: Creates objects by inspecting constructor requirements, injecting container services, config maps, well-known service aliases, and default values where appropriate.

feature3_3_reflection_factory_errors.jso.description: Reports normalized dependency-resolution errors when automatic constructor inspection encounters required parameters that cannot be supplied.

feature4_1_plugin_manager_validation_and.description: A constrained child container retrieves valid plugin objects, caches normal lookups, bypasses the cache when options are supplied, and rejects invalid plugin instances.

feature5_1_container_decorator.json.description: Wraps a standards-compatible container so existence checks, retrieval, and access to the wrapped container are forwarded transparently.
